results.possibleIdentity: Masaaki Sekiya (also known as Sekiya Masaaki) is a Japanese photographer and artist who was active in the 1960s and 1970s. He is closely associated with the history of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) and the avant-garde scene in Tokyo, particularly the Sogetsu Art Center. Sekiya is renowned for documenting the early collaborations between Japanese and American artists and engineers. His notable work includes photographing the 1964 Merce Cunningham Dance Company world tour in Tokyo, which featured E.A.T. founders Robert Rauschenberg and Billy Klüver. His photographs are essential historical records of pivotal technological art events like '9 Evenings: Theatre and Engineering' and the 'Pepsi Pavilion' at Expo '70, and they are frequently featured in retrospectives at institutions such as the Museum of Modern Art (MoMA) and the Solomon R. Guggenheim Museum.

Masaaki Sato (born 1941) is a Japanese conceptual artist who emigrated to New York in 1970 and has been active through the late 1960s and 1970s to the present. He is known for his 'Holes' series of paintings and prints, which explore visual perception and depth. His name appears in museum catalogs and exhibitions alongside 'Experiments in Art and Technology, Inc.' and other figures of the New York and Japanese avant-garde. His work is represented in major collections, including the Brooklyn Museum and the Portland Art Museum. Sato's career is characterized by his involvement in the community of Japanese artists in New York who experimented with moving images and new conceptual frameworks during the late 20th century.

results.possibleIdentity: Russell Dian is a New York-based photographer and artist who has been active since the late 1960s or early 1970s. He is closely associated with the early video art movement and technical-artistic collaborations of that era. He provided photography for the influential journal Radical Software, which was published by the Raindance Foundation starting in 1970. Radical Software was a primary forum for the alternate television and video art community, frequently intersecting with the goals of organizations like Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.). Throughout his career, Dian has worked as a professional photographer, with credits in numerous publications and for various artists, including portraits of contemporary classical composers like Faye-Ellen Silverman. He is married to the artist Alicia Benoist, and the couple has participated in mixed-media art exhibitions together, such as the 2018 show 'What's the Matter?' in Atlantic Highlands, New Jersey, where Dian's photography was featured.

results.possibleIdentity: Robert Sterling Neuman (1926–2015) was a prominent American abstract painter, printmaker, and art educator active from the early 1950s through the 2010s. He was a significant figure in the second generation of Abstract Expressionists and was highly active during the late 1960s and early 1970s. During this period, he taught at Harvard University's Carpenter Center for Visual Arts (1965–1972) and later chaired the art department at Keene State College (1972–1990). His work evolved from early gestural abstraction into what critics called "emblematic abstraction," characterized by the use of symbols, geometric forms, and a vibrant color palette. Notable series from the 1960s and 1970s include "Pedazos del Mundo" (Pieces of the World), which riffed on themes of urbanization and cosmic order, and "Space Signs," which explored the era's fascination with the space race. While his tenure at the Carpenter Center placed him at a hub for visual and technological experimentation, his specific association with the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization is not explicitly documented in common historical records, though his thematic interests in space and global systems aligned with the technological interests of that era's avant-garde.

Robert Neuman is an art historian and Professor Emeritus at Florida State University who specializes in the intersection of art and popular culture, specifically the history of Disney theme parks and animation. He is the author of "From Hollywood to Disneyland: Walt Disney's Dream Park and the Influence of American Movies" (2022). His scholarship frequently examines how Disney utilized experimental technology and architectural design to create "cinematic" and immersive environments. Although he is a scholar rather than a practicing artist from the 1960s, his work frequently analyzes the history of technology-based art and projects like Disney's "Experimental Prototype Community of Tomorrow" (EPCOT), which shares conceptual ground with the goals of the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization.

Robert Neuman is a contemporary stereoscopic specialist and animator at Walt Disney Animation Studios, recognized for his expertise in 3D film technology and stereography. He has served as a stereoscopic supervisor on major films such as "Bolt" (2008) and "Wreck-It Ralph" (2012). His work involves the application of scientific principles of parallax and depth perception to artistic storytelling. In academic and technical literature, he is often cited in discussions regarding the history of art-technology collaborations, sometimes in proximity to the legacy of the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) group, as his work represents a modern continuation of the integration of engineering and visual arts.
